sentences.text: I should have described to you when I mentioned that we managed to somehow smuggle in some food and some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] medication, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] mostly we did it in the casket when we took the dead out.
As you know, in Orthodox cemeteries, particularly, people are buried in their shrouds without actual caskets.
And we smuggled the food and medication in, in the ostensibly empty basket on the way back from the burials.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that really was one of the main sources.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were a few other ways in which we could manage to obtain one thing or another, but very little.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, it-- in one, one respect, it was time that was against us.
We never had the time to organize or to plan.
Well, we were actually told that we were going to be deported, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at least the Jewish Council was told.
And of course, everybody got to know it.

And then at one point, we were again told that we have to prepare for-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they didn't call it deportation, of course-- evacuation, or some other Aesopian language, on a certain date, which I gathered that in our case-- and I don't know whether I was in the first or the second transport from Kisvarda-- was in, on May 29, 1944.
It was a pretty messy affair.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- well, we all supposedly had to present ourselves just at the front of the, of the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People were slowly coming early in the morning with whatever the weight allowance.

I, I don't know what it was, because for days before that, people were all sewing all sorts of rucksack-type things that they could and, and suitcases and, and bags and sacks to squeeze in whatever they thought would help them to save themselves.

So whatever they thought they would need or could use, not, of course, realizing that that was really part of the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] of the German way of lulling people into believing that there was something beyond what was, and maybe even better.

It was a, a really elementary kind of psychology that they practiced, which was so useful to them in cowing the population that was to become the subject of their murder, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a human psychology such that you will not only cling to any kind of hope, but create elaborate scenarios for how it is going to be better even than it is, let alone not worse.

The notion when they tell you that you have to pack, I don't know, 50 kilograms of-- and a week-long food for the trip, it, it makes you think that there is a trip, that you'll be traveling, that you will be eating, that, that you will change your clothes, and that there will be a toilet or, or-- it, it, it makes you think that there will be a, perhaps a, a, an inconvenience, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but not utter and complete devastation.
East.
The word was always east.
Evacuation to the east, where all the Jews may have been brought together, where the enemy isn't going to reach us.
After all, we were in real danger, you know.
The Russians may come any minute.
And what could be worse than that?
Well, the, the Germans would-- that, that sole German officer and the gendarmerie would present themselves to the council.
And they would tell the council that on such and such date there will be a evacuation, and you have to tell the population that they should prepare themselves for in such and such a way.
And they would put up placards in key spots in a-- in the ghetto.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the fact that they, that they told you that you can take food and clothing along or that you should was sufficient to calm your worst fears.
And this is the kind of simple elementary psychology that the Germans practiced all over, in the concentration camps, everyplace.
It isn't the gullibility of human beings.

It is, rather, the desperate need of human beings to cling to, to life and to cling to the positive, the hopeful which was exploited so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sure that at certain levels, it was very conscious and very planful on the part of the Germans, but you really didn't have to be conscious and planful about it.

If you were a German SS man and you were told from higher ups that you announced that there was going to be a evacuation and the, and the evacuees will be allowed to take 50 kilograms each of clothing and--
What?
Well, [SIGHS] I, I know a few things that I was very particular about.
One was I had a pair of boots, very heavy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mountain-climbing boots from my days in, in Switzerland.
And I was very sure to take that.
And you know, we all tried to get-- mind you, this was going into the summer.
But everybody tried to take some winter clothing along, because if, well, we're going to be evacuated east, east is cold, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, people tried to stuff in as much food as they could lay their hands on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody had different ideas of what was advisable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's--
So you know, people began to pour out of these-- the homes in the ghetto where they were onto this [?
Star ?] Street.
And by that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the sentry were more directly under the control of the, of the gendarmes, because they never came inside the ghetto.
They, so to say, ruled through the Jewish Council and by guarding the perimeter.
But here they began to drive us with considerable brutality.
[INAUDIBLE] my sister has-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my sister Izabella has actually described it in, in her book.
We were being squeezed into a line.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is-- I never in my life want to hear the word 'five in a row,' because that seemed to have been fixed in the minds of the Germans.
We had to line up five to a row.
And they were shoving and pushing and shouting.
And, and total disarray, of course, and chaos, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because, you know, the lame and the hurt and the, and the dumb and the, and the blind.
And they couldn't be told to keep order and to stay in line, then they would lash out at them.
And apparently, this young SS man had a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] whip, and he lashed out with a whip at my mother.
And--
--I was-- what?
Yeah, he lashed at, at, out at my mother and I.
Apparently, I was just about to jump him, and the girls, Izabella and Chicha, with all the force that, that they could manage, they held me back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because they-- I, in my blind rage, didn't-- but they realized that we would all be dead or my mother would be killed in front of our eyes.
So they held me back.
And it became a, for, for some moments, a, a very angry encounter between myself and my sisters.
No.
Never met him before and never saw him after.
Anyway, we somehow-- they managed to, to, to drive us and to whip us till what was called in, in Kisvarda-- we had two rail stations.
One was called the big one and the other one was called the small one, the small station.
And the small station had a spur going into a lumber yard of an, of another Jew, not the one that I had mentioned before.
But there was a huge grassed area, you know, of, of the yard.
And there were the cattle cars.
And we were being shoved into it and, and beaten into it and forced into it.
There were a couple of people who managed to escape from there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, again, from our vantage point, this was total chaos.
Clearly, we were not an organized group.
And-- but, but the Germans and the gendarmes, they knew exactly what they were doing.
They were, they were whipping us into the, into the thing.
Can I recall my-- yes, sure.
How do I make my mother and my sisters as comfortable as humanly possible?
How do I get everything that we thought we may need?
All the bags, many of them, you know, they just thrown about, either almost voluntarily or because they were being driven and beaten into the, into the cars, and they just lost it.
People run back to try to catch it.
And  [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- personally, my whole preoccupation was with how do I make my family as, as-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] how do I make it as bearable as possible?
Approximately how many people were in this cattle car with you?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We propped up our mother against in one corner with my little sister in front of her and all of us, all my sisters, sort of surrounding them, barely enough room to stand.
And people would fall down.
And you know, people died in, in the car.
We were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] on our way for, I think, three nights.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we crossed what used to be the old Czech border or a little past it, there was a town called Kosice, K-O-S-I-C-I-E in, in Slovakian, but in, in Hungarian, it was known as Kassa, K-A-- K-A-S-S-A.
And there the Hungarian police formally handed us over to the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The, the trip was probably the most inhuman aspect of, of the Holocaust.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] impossible.
I don't know how any of us survived it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in Poland, and I'm trying to remember now what the name of the town was where we stood, where the train was halted for whatever the reason.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had spoken to one of the guards.
There was a guard to every train, to every cattle car.
He was standing below.
And we had just enough of a slit in the, in the one window that you usually have on these cattle cars, which by the way, was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] boarded up.
And I had broken it out so we could get a little bit of air.
Other than that, there was no air at all.
And I spoke to the, to this SS man and handed him some rings and some money.
And we got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the car opened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went down to get some water.
And then it seems to me that it must have been that he had either decided on his own or they were going to do it anyway, some of the other cars were also opened.
And like myself, some of the younger people jumped out.
And there was, you know, water for the train.

And we collected some water in whatever dishes and pails we-- people had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and emptied out some of the pails that we used for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the car-- for voiding, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which, which, you know, was also, I presume, happened in every car.
But it was a spontaneous idea of one of us, cause there was no, there were no facilities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we got a little water.
I remember I had already taken off my shirt, and I let the water run all over me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we had a little water for each of the cars.
Probably lasted 10 minutes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we arrived in, in the morning in Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to-- again, this is very interesting, you know.
To us, it all seemed total chaos and disarray.
I mean, we were pouring out of the railroad cars by the thousands.

And there were, you know, maybe a half a dozen SS men with dogs hanging around, but there were-- and there were these peculiar people with the striped suits rushing around and shouting, get out and out.
And, and the dogs were barking.
And, and it all seemed like total chaos, and yet the Germans had very good order.
Without even knowing, they were separating men and women, you know, without-- we, we didn't even realize it.
We didn't have a chance to realize it.
It all went like a well-oiled machine, even though that to us it looked chaotic.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And these funny-looking striped people would drop a word here and a word there or motion, you're going up into smoke.
And when you tried to hang on to something that you had brought, because they would tear it out of your hand and take it away.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they would, they would tell you, you're as good as dead, and all sorts of hints, drops, outright statements, and it all had to be done in, in seconds, because before you knew it, you were, you were already ten paces away.
You were being shoved and pushed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] all towards one, one goal, towards Dr.
Mengele.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we didn't know.
You were just being literally pushed without your awareness of where you were going or why you were going.
The sheer force of being driven [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and being shoved brought you there.
Certainly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I have a feeling that the fact that I had-- I think it was Sosnowiec, but I'm not sure where the, the train had stopped and I had, had torn off my shirt.
It was not only foul in those cars, but there was no air.
And it was hot, even hotter than under this lamp.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I guess a, a, a strong, young body-- I was pretty muscly-- is what Mengele saw, so he sent me to the life side.
I didn't know.
I knew after I got in to the camp where everybody knew.
I didn't know.
I don't even think that I-- that we realized, really, that this was what later we knew was selection.
I mean, this man stood there with white gloves, and he just went like this.
And you didn't even have a chance to look at him, because you were being shoved and pushed.
And the din and the noise and, and, and the barking of the dogs and, and the thing being hauled away.
And the train was being shoved back and forth and was screeching.
It-- you know, you-- you had no awareness of, of your surroundings.
You had no cognition.
You, you knew that you were moving, but you didn't know where or, or how or, or who pushed you or who shoved you.
I saw her being driven [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with other women.
And you know, that, too, it was like automatically, they formed into a line of five in a row.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then subsequently, I passed by [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I saw [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] her pajamas.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] And you know, you, you lost awareness of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yourself or anyone else or anything, because you were on a, on a conveyor belt.
You were constantly-- your, your circumstances were constantly changing violently.
I mean, here you were, you just passed Dr.
Mengele, and you were already marching forward.
And, and, and incidentally, he-- I don't exactly now know at what hour we arrived, but by the time I got to where we were eventually put into the showers, after the fashion, it was that night.
Believe me, I didn't notice that time has gone by and, and it went from day to night.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Time had all flown together, flowed together, and, and, and became one unrecognizable and undistinguishable and inseparable process, a kind of a, a, a flow [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] without knowing certainly any difference between one hour and, and one day or one month or one year.
It's, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was an incredibly well-organized and efficient procedure that was elementary, and perhaps, because of that, so effective.
And everything about it was elementary.
I passed through Dr.
Mengele.
In front of me was the youngest brother of a lifelong friend of mine who lives in the United States.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I do not know to this day-- and this is, again, part of the same problem, in a way, of the time.
You know, Mengele was going like this.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether or not he didn't point him to go to the life side and me to the wrong side.
And because the thing was moving so fast, by the time his finger moved to this side, I had run past the other side.
I don't know.
I always have a question in my mind, whether or not.
And, and most people have problems like that.
I shouldn't say most people, but a lot of people have problems like that, whether they did not go to the, to the so-called life side in place of somebody else.
Who knows?
It was all one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] miasmic mess.
It was--
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, the, the real truths or one of the real truths about the Holocaust is that it is like God's name.
It's ineffable.
It can't be expressed.
It can't be described.
And it can't be communicated.
I know it.
You can never know it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is beyond the ken of human beings who have lived maybe a not very good life, but have lived in-- on the planet Earth.
We come from another planet.
Sure.
